coredata.dc:title: Microplasma-assisted synthesis and regulation the luminescence performance of glucose-based carbon dots by sodium borohydride
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tyutrin, A.A.
Rakevich, A.L.
Martynovich, E.F.
coredata.dc:description: The effect of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) on the luminescent properties of glucose-based carbon dots synthesized by the plasma method is investigated. The spectral and kinetic properties of the carbon dots were studied using a MicroTime 200 confocal scanning fluorescence microscope. The lower concentration limit of NaBH4 was established at which there is an enhancement of the CDs luminescence intensity. It was found that adding of sodium borohydride to the CDs solution at concentration below 2.5 mg/mL leads to an increase in the luminescence intensity, while a decrease in intensity is noted at concentrations above 2.5 mg/mL. Enhancement of the luminescence intensity by 2.8 times was detected for the sample with the mass fraction of NaBH4 was 0.04 % in the solution. Using the time-correlated photon counting method, it was shown that the luminescence decay kinetics is described by a tri-exponential function. It has been experimentally demonstrated that the fast luminescence time component is related to the carbonyl functional group on the carbon dots surface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Carbon dots
Sodium borohydride
Time-correlated single photon counting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic synthesis of CDs by plasma method.
TEM image (a) and Raman spectra (b) of CDs.
Photographs of CDs solutions without NaBH4 (a) and with NaBH4 concentrations of 0.5 (b), 1 (c), 2.5 (d), 25 (e) mg/mL, respectively, under laser excitation at 405 nm.
Luminescence spectra of the CDs solution without NaBH4 (CD) and with NaBH4 concentration of 0.5 (rCD1), 1 (rCD2), 2.5 (rCD3), 25 (rCD4) mg/mL, respectively, under laser excitation at 375 nm.
Absorption spectra of CDs solution without NaBH4 (CD) and with NaBH4 concentration of 0.5 (rCD1), 1 (rCD2), 2.5 (rCD3), 25 (rCD4) mg/mL.
FTIR spectra of the CDs before (CD) and after reduction (rCD1, rCD2, rCD3, rCD4) by NaBH4.
Luminescence decay kinetics of CDs solution without NaBH4 (a) and with NaBH4 concentrations of 0.5 (b), 1 (c), 2.5 (d), 25 (e) mg/mL, respectively,
Luminescent properties of carbon dots before and after reduction by NaBH4.

The decay time constants (τ) of three luminescence time components and fraction of the intensity (A) for each time component CDs solution depending on the concentration of NaBH4 under laser excitation at 375 nm.

head.ce:title: Microplasma-assisted synthesis and regulation the luminescence performance of glucose-based carbon dots by sodium borohydride

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Irkutsk Branch of Institute of Laser Physics, Siberian Branch, Russian Academy of Sciences, Irkutsk 664033, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Irkutsk Branch of Institute of Laser Physics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effect of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) on the luminescent properties of glucose-based carbon dots synthesized by the plasma method is investigated. The spectral and kinetic properties of the carbon dots were studied using a MicroTime 200 confocal scanning fluorescence microscope. The lower concentration limit of NaBH4 was established at which there is an enhancement of the CDs luminescence intensity. It was found that adding of sodium borohydride to the CDs solution at concentration below 2.5 mg/mL leads to an increase in the luminescence intensity, while a decrease in intensity is noted at concentrations above 2.5 mg/mL. Enhancement of the luminescence intensity by 2.8 times was detected for the sample with the mass fraction of NaBH4 was 0.04 % in the solution. Using the time-correlated photon counting method, it was shown that the luminescence decay kinetics is described by a tri-exponential function. It has been experimentally demonstrated that the fast luminescence time component is related to the carbonyl functional group on the carbon dots surface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoparticle (<10 nm) of carbon materials with quasi-spherical morphology that have received significant scientific attention since their discovery in 2004 [1]. CDs are photostable, biocompatible with the tissues of living organisms, non-toxic compared to traditional quantum dots and have a low cost price. Due to these properties, they are actively used in optoelectronics [2,3], bioimaging [4,5], sensors [6,7], photocatalysis [8–10].

There are many methods for synthesizing nanoparticles, which can be divided into two categories: Top-down and Bottom-up methods [11]. Each of these methods has its pros and cons. Some synthesis methods require expensive equipment to achieve high temperatures and pressures, as well as hazardous chemical reagents, which require special qualifications and permission to work with. In this regard, plasma methods of nanoparticle synthesis are a good alternative to traditional methods for creating new functional materials and electronic devices. The advantages of the plasma method include simplicity, speed and environmental friendliness of nanoparticle synthesis, as well as the ability to work at atmospheric pressure [12–16].

Plasma synthesis of CDs is a new approach that allows to reach conditions, completely different, for example, from the traditional hydrothermal synthesis method, to form carbon dots with unique properties [17,18]. Plasma facilitates the formation of a wide range of radicals, including various reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS and RNS). The interaction of reactive radicals formed in the plasma with the material contributes to the formation of a wide variety of functional groups on the CDs surface, which play an important role in luminescence processes. In our work [19] it was shown that the luminescence of carbon dots synthesized by the plasma method from a glucose solution is mainly due to surface functional groups, and not to the quantum confinement effect.

Quantum yield is one of the important parameters of fluorescence of carbon dots, which largely determines the possibility of their use in optical applications. Modern research is focused on the formation of CDs with a high quantum yield of luminescence, as well as on methods for its regulation. For this purpose, various synthesis methods [20–22], the influence of numerous precursors and chemical reagents on the properties of CDs during synthesis and post-processing [23–25] are investigated.

Formation of certain functional groups on the surface of carbon dots can be an effective approach to control the luminescence properties. One approach to controlling the luminescence properties of carbon dots is through chemical modification using redox processes [26,27]. The essence of the method lies in the addition of oxidizing or reducing agents to the solution containing already synthesized particles or during the synthesis process. These agents transform or disrupt specific surface states, which, in turn, play a crucial role in the luminescence processes. The surface of carbon dots is rich by functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, epoxy groups, etc. Changes in the functional groups on the surface of carbon dots significantly affect their luminescent properties. Traditionally, nitric acid (HNO₃) is used as an oxidizing agent, while reducing agents may include ascorbic acid (C₆H₈O₆), sodium sulfide (Na₂S), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) and others. Usually, the addition of oxidants results in a decrease in the luminescence intensity of carbon dots, while the introduction of reducing agents such as ascorbic acid, sodium sulfide and sodium citrate does not lead to a noticeable increase in the luminescence intensity [28].

However, in studies focused on enhancing the quantum yield of carbon dots, the use of another reducing agent - sodium borohydride (NaBH4) is increasingly being reported. Sodium borohydride is a reagent that selectively reduced the carbonyl to a hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs without reducing other functional groups (CC, COOH) [29].

In [28], it was shown that the addition of sodium borohydride to a solution containing carbon dots synthesized via the hydrothermal method led to an increase in the luminescence quantum yield from 2 % to 24 %, as well as to a shift in the luminescence peak from 520 nm to 450 nm. In another work [30], the addition of sodium borohydride to the CDs solution, also synthesized via the hydrothermal method, led to an increase in the quantum yield from 35.73 % to 44.61 %. It is worth noting that the luminescence quantum yield increased proportionally with an increase in the concentration of sodium borohydride. Similar results with an increase in the luminescence quantum yield of CDs were obtained in works [31–36]. The luminescent properties of carbon dots before and after reduction by NaBH4 are presented in Table 1 .

As shown above, the use of sodium borohydride as a reducing agent can contribute to an increase in the luminescence intensity of the CDs. However, the quantum yield of the CDs luminescence varies significantly depending on the synthesis procedure, precursors, reagents, and the concentration of sodium borohydride. In addition, some studies report a shift in the position of the luminescence peak of CDs, while others do not observe such a shift. Consequently, the effect of sodium borohydride on the luminescent properties of CDs will vary in each specific case. Thus, the purpose of this work is to investigate the effect of sodium borohydride on the luminescent properties of carbon dots synthesized by the plasma method from a glucose solution.

The experimental setup for the plasma synthesis of CDs is shown in Fig. 1 . A 40 % glucose solution (10 mL) was filled into a special glass cuvette. Cylindrical graphite rod 50 mm long and 5 mm in diameter was immersed in the solution at one end of the cuvette. A sharp stainless steel electrode was placed at the other end of the cuvette at a distance of 1–2 mm above the solution surface. High voltage was applied to the electrodes, resulting in the formation of microplasma on the steel electrode. The plasma was formed at atmospheric pressure in air. The microplasma treatment duration was 60 min for all samples. After that, CDs was filtered from large fractions using membrane filters with a pore size of ∼0.22 μm. The choice of glucose as a precursor is explained by the fact that it is environmentally friendly, non-toxic and biocompatible with the tissues of organisms. Description and explanation of the synthesis of CDs by the plasma method, as well as the structural and luminescent parameters, are presented in the study [19].

The sample labeled CD represents a solution containing carbon dots synthesized by the plasma method for 1 h. It serves as a reference against which comparisons are made. Following plasma treatment, four samples - rCD1, rCD2, rCD3, and rCD4, were prepared with varying concentrations of sodium borohydride - 0.5, 1, 2.5, 25 mg/mL, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (LEO 906E) operated at 120 kV was used to determine the morphological and microstructural properties of the prepared CDs. Raman spectroscopy was carried out using an Ocean Optics QE65000 spectrometer and an 808 nm laser as an excitation source. FTIR spectra were obtained using a IRAffinity-1 (Shimadzu).

The luminescence characteristics of the samples were measured using a confocal scanning fluorescence microscope with time resolution MicroTime 200 (PicoQuant GmbH), equipped with an Ocean Optics QE65000 spectrometer. The luminescence spectra and kinetics of the carbon dots are presented under excitation at a wavelength of 375 nm, as this wavelength corresponds to the maximum luminescence intensity [19].

The TEM image and Raman spectra of the CDs are presented in Fig. 2 . CDs exhibit a spherical shape (Fig. 2a), with sizes ranging from 1 to 8 nm. The average diameter was determined to be 4.8 nm, which is in good agreement with the result obtained in [19].

Fig. 2b displays the Raman spectrum of CDs. The Raman spectrum of carbon dots exhibits two vibrational modes appearing at 1365 (D band) and 1570 cm−1 (G band). The D-band indicates the presence of sp3-disordered carbon in the carbon dot structure actuated by symmetry breaking at defects and edges. The G-band arises due to in-plane stretching of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. It reflects the presence of ordered graphitic domains. It can be seen the intensity of the D band was lower than that of the G band, exhibiting the dominating plenty of sp2, with the addition of sp3‑carbon atoms.

Fig. 3 shows photographs of carbon dot solutions without the addition of NaBH4 and with NaBH4 concentrations of 0.5 (b), 1 (c), 2.5 (d), 25 (e) mg/mL, respectively, under laser excitation at 405 nm. During photography of the solutions, a light filter was used on the camera lens to cut off the excitation radiation. The photographs clearly show the influence of sodium borohydride concentration on the luminescence intensity of CDs.

Fig. 4 shows the luminescence spectra of CDs with different NaBH4 concentrations excited by laser radiation at a wavelength of 375 nm. For convenience, the NaBH4 concentration is also indicated in units of the mass fraction percent (Fig. 4). The luminescence peak maximum for all samples is located around ∼470 nm, and no spectral shift is observed upon addition of sodium borohydride. The maximum luminescence intensity was recorded for the rCD1 sample, which had the lowest concentration of sodium borohydride in the solution. The ratios of the luminescence peak intensities of the samples with NaBH4 (I) to the CD sample (I0) are as follows: I/I0 values: 2.8, 1.87, 1.19, 0.48, in order of increasing NaBH4 concentration, respectively. From the data in Fig. 4, it can be concluded that the addition of sodium borohydride to the carbon dot solution at concentrations below 2.5 mg/mL gradually increases the luminescence intensity, while concentrations above 2.5 mg/mL lead to a decrease in luminescence intensity.

Fig. 5 shows the absorption spectra for the CD, rCD1, rCD2, rCD3, rCD4 samples. The absorption peaks at 250 nm wavelength correspond to the π → π* transition in the CC bond and around 303 nm wavelength is for n → π* transition in the CO bond [37]. With increasing NaBH4 concentration in solutions, the optical density of the band at the 303 nm peak gradually decreases, as the reagent reduce the carbonyl group (C=O) into hydroxyl (OH). At a high concentration of NaBH4, a slight spectral shift from 303.4 nm to 309 nm (rCD4) is observed. A similar shift is observed in the article [36].

The results of the experiments investigating the effect of NaBH4 on the luminescent properties of CDs were repeatedly verified. In all cases, an increase in the luminescence intensity of carbon dots is observed at NaBH4 concentrations below 2.5 mg/mL, while a decrease in intensity is noted at concentrations above 2.5 mg/mL, with the corresponding optical density of the reference sample as in Fig. 5.

In order to investigate the presence of various functional groups on the surface of glucose based CDs the FTIR spectra were measured (Fig. 6 ). FTIR results showed the presence of a large number of functional groups on the CDs surface such as a broad absorption band of the OH stretching vibrations (3288 cm−1), CH (2887 cm−1), CO (1715 cm−1), CC (1665 cm−1) and C-O-C (1036 cm−1) stretching peaks. The obtained FTIR spectra are very similar but after the reduction by NaBH4, a CO stretching peak (1715 cm−1) decreased. No significant increase in the intensity of the hydroxyl group on the CDs surface is observed.

The luminescence decay kinetics are also an important parameter for studying the optical properties of CDs. For this purpose, in our work we used the method of time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC), which allowed us to decompose luminescence into components of the decay time with the contribution of photons in each component. All measurements of the luminescence decay kinetics were carried out at the same intensity of laser excitation.

Since sodium borohydride directly acts on the carbonyl group, a decrease in the number of bonds of this group is observed in all samples, which is clearly reflected in the FTIR and absorption spectra (Figs. 5 and 6) of the carbon dots and in Table 2. In [38], it was shown that the carbonyl group on the CDs surface is directly related to non-radiative recombination, which leads to luminescence quenching. Accordingly, an increase in the luminescence intensity of CDs at NaBH4 concentrations below 2.5 mg/mL is related to the transformation of the carbonyl group into a hydroxyl group by replacing electron-withdrawing groups with electron-donating groups, i.e. it is related to a decrease in the CO bonds on the surface of CDs. The reduction of CO to -OH groups minimizes surface defects, reduces non-radiative recombination, and enhances radiative recombination, leading to a higher fluorescence quantum yield. However, at high NaBH4 concentrations, structural changes occur in CDs, which are reflected in a significant change in the decay time constants, as well as in a slight shift in the absorption spectrum from 303 to 309 nm (Fig. 5). Presumably, additional non-radiative decay channels are formed in CDs. The luminescence quenching at high concentrations of NaBH4 was also observed in [39,40].

The luminescent properties of carbon dots synthesized by the plasma method using glucose as a precursor were investigated depending on the sodium borohydride concentration. The luminescence intensity of carbon dots increased after reduction at NaBH4 concentration below 2.5 mg/mL. It is explained by a decrease in the number of carbonyl bonds on the CDs surface, which otherwise cause luminescence quenching through non-radiative recombination. The decrease in the luminescence intensity of CDs is observed at NaBH4 concentration above 2.5 mg/mL, which can be attributed to significant structural changes in the CDs. The ratios of the luminescence peak intensities of the samples with NaBH4 to the CD sample have the following I/I0 values: 2.8, 1.87, 1.19, 0.48 in the order of increasing NaBH4 concentration, respectively. Using the time-correlated single photon counting method it was shown that the observed luminescence can be well fitted with a tri-exponential function. It was experimentally demonstrated that the fast component (τ1) of the luminescence is related to the carbonyl functional group on the CDs surface. Practical recommendations were obtained for regulating the CDs luminescence intensity synthesized by the plasma method using glucose as precursor.

A.A. Tyutrin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. A.L. Rakevich: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology. E.F. Martynovich: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Russian Federation as part of the Fundamental Research Plan of the Russian Academy of Sciences (project No. 0243-2021-0004) and the state assignment (project No. FZZE-2023-0004 and FZZE-2023-0005).
